If you're in real estate investing, cold calling is THE best way to source the best deals 💰 My name is David Choi, I'm a real estate investor and fund manager with over $50,000,000 in AUM, and here are 7 psychology tricks to DOMINATE cold calling real estate.

--
David Choi is a real estate investor and fund manager with +$50,000,000 in AUM and has closed over +700 transactions. He is Founder and Managing Partner of Leverage Companies, a real estate private equity company located in the heart of downtown Newark. 

As President of Leverage Homes, David has overseen over +700 real estate transactions with a sum total of $350,000,000 of transaction volume focused primarily in the state of New Jersey. 

As Managing Partner of Brick City Capital, David has helped facilitate over $500,000,000 in funding in its first two years of business. After starting the TLC Opportunity Debt Fund I, David has helped raise over $15,000,000 in investor capital and is currently on track to fund over $600,000,000 in loan volume annually.

As Managing Partner of Leverage Holdings, David currently manages +250 units and $60,000,000 of AUM in multifamily apartments across New Jersey and New York state. The company is on track to manage +2,500 units by the year 2028.

As the founder of Leverage Media, David has become an international phenomenon in the real estate education space. Accruing over 125,000,000 views and 700,000 followers across social media, David has been a tastemaker and trend setter in the world of reality content creation. After founding his Deals & Dollars Academy, he has offered mentorship and real estate knowledge to thousands of fans and students.

Aside from his work with Leverage Companies, David is an Adjunct Professor of Real Estate Finance at the Rutgers Business School in Newark, NJ. He is honored to be teaching at his alma mater, where he graduated Cum Laude in 2019 with a degree in Finance with a concentration in Real Estate.

Dave's advice on the incremental transition and apprenticeship model is spot-on. Here's what I'd add as someone who works with real estate agents on operational systems: the 'grunt work' Dave mentions isn't just learning to show houses, it's learning the infrastructure. CRM for lead follow-up, transaction tracking, client communication workflows, digital marketing systems. Most new agents skip this and wonder why they're drowning six months in. If you're joining a high-production team, pay attention to their systems, not just their sales tactics. Ask: How do they track leads? What's their follow-up cadence? How do they manage transactions from contract to close? Document everything. The agents who scale aren't just good at closing deals—they're good at building repeatable processes. I use Go High Level to build this infrastructure for agents, but the platform doesn't matter as much as the discipline to design workflows before you need them. Start with systems, not hustle. You'll thank yourself later.

All across America, real estate prep companies and brokerages are taking peoples' money, knowing full well the real estate industry is saturated with too many realtors. There are millions of agents in America, because it's so easy to get licensed. How is anyone suppose to make money in an industry like this? Most agents don't even care about different types of houses, and they can't tell one house from another. Speaking of houses, the number of houses to buy and sell is significantly low and the vast majority of homes in America are now so poorly constructed. Modern homes look the same and buyers are complaining about America's overpriced, cookie-cutter houses. Where is a realtor suppose to muster up the motivation to sell homes that are such poor quality? Realtors who earn a little money spend far more on lead generating and marketing than they get in return. 

The only realtors who I see making this job worth their time are people who enter the real estate industry already with a large social circle of wealthy friends and relatives with great credit, no school loan debt, and unlimited purchasing power. Veteran realtors, living in privileged communities, will also pass on their large databases to their kids who then become realtors. Most agents do not have access to exclusive social circles full of wealthy people who can afford to purchase homes that would generate a high enough commission to be worth the time and money realtors invest in their careers. I would not recommend this job to anyone who cares about making an efficient use of his or her time.

Selling products that aren't 'needs' and more of a 'want' is the difficult part. Especially in todays economic climate.

1. The pursuit/prospecting:
    1. Find the best customers (fit your offer)
    2. Know well their needs
    3. Connect
        1. Introduction
        2. Speak with the right people
        3. Schedule a meeting
2. The alliance
    1. Create trust
        1. Dress well
        2. Eye contact
        3. Smile/Enthusiast to sell what you have
        4. Encourage them to talk about themselves
3. The pitch
    1. You need to understand their situation
        1. What the pain point: Things they can’t achieve, what you can offer them 
        2. Price point : What their limit versus yours to maximise gains and minimize losses 
        3. Who is the decision maker ? Save your time
    2. You need to close pitch with
        1. Deliverables 
        2. Key stakeholders
        3. Timeframe
4. The demo : show them that you can provide them what they need
    1. Example : portfolio, camera, ipad, clients, projets, equipement, inclure ton entourage
5. Negotiating/ Objection:
    1. Product, time, money, stall or other
        1. Product : What can be something you are looking for
        2. Money: What could be you budget ? Adjust the time/effort put in work to find middle ground 
    2. First step, agree with the prospect
    3. Is there other reason you wouldn’t go forward, isolate the issue
6. Close the sell
    1. Follow up
    2. Follow through
    3. Follow Back

- 00:00🎯 Today, we're discussing a six-step sales process that I use to close deals and grow my billion-dollar business.
- 00:20🚀 Introducing the Sell It Sales Cycle: a dynamic, repeatable six-step system to enhance your sales strategy.
- 00:32📚 Applicable to various industries, from real estate to Girl Scout cookies, to help hit sales quotas or grow a business.
- 00:46🛠 Our new sales course offers detailed training, templates, webinars, and tools for sales success.
- 01:11🔑 Master the six steps: The Pursuit, The Alliance, Light the Spark, Make Your Move, The Wrap-Up, and The Art of the Follow-Up.
- 01:28🔍 Step 1: The Pursuit involves prospecting, identifying your ideal customer profile (ICP), and understanding customer needs.
- 01:52🧩 Determine who benefits most from your services and analyze your top customers' demographics and behavior.
- 02:26📞 Effective outreach: connect with prospects via email, DM, or cold call, starting with an introduction and moving to a hook.
- 03:04🤝 Step 2: The Alliance focuses on building trust and security with prospects for long-lasting business relationships.
- 03:20🌟 First impressions matter: dress well, make eye contact, smile, and show authentic enthusiasm for what you're selling.
- 04:02💡 Step 3: Light the Spark involves uncovering the client's true needs through discovery to tailor a compelling pitch.
- 04:11📞 Ask about their pain points, price point, and whether they are the decision-maker to better address their needs.
- 05:01🎬 Step 4: Make Your Move, the demo, involves showcasing how you can meet the client's needs with a well-executed presentation.
- 05:27📊 Use visual aids, stories, and testimonials to make your demo engaging and memorable, focusing on solving the client's main problem.
- 05:56🤝 Step 5: The Wrap-up involves negotiation and closing the deal by handling objections and crafting a win-win proposal.
- 07:03🔄 Step 6: The Art of the Follow-Up ensures continued client engagement with follow-up, follow-through, and follow-back techniques.
- 07:19📞 Check in with clients regularly, follow through on promises, and reconnect with past or lost clients to maintain relationships.
- 07:49📈 The Sell It Sales Cycle aims to create lifelong clients, emphasizing that closing a sale is just the beginning of the relationship.

I have many critiques on marketing. 

Number 1: Professional Photographers. Waste of time and money. Most high end phones have the technology to snap an accurate and beautiful photo. My recommendation is pay attention to angles and lighting. That's the most important thing. 

2. Social Media: Lets be honest, powerful tool, right? However, unless you have investors following you, it's unnecessary. Definitely post it, and get a following because it can definitely help. But! You can go without it.

3. Paid Ads: Often times a waste of money. Most people use 3rd party apps to search for properties. Now, what do they do? Filter Price, Filter Specs, Filter Location and Search. When a person is actively shopping, and have a Realtor working with them, that buyer agent will pull the properties that meet their needs and price range and present them. Paid ads often work for upcoming communities, events and mass showings to attract a massive crowd. 

What Realtors need to do is tell their clients the truth. If you want to sell, you have to understand the market and the needs of the buyer. Competitive Price, Good Photos, Good Description and timely showings (Open Houses). That will get your property sold much quicker than any of the above.

FSBO is not for everyone, but it's definitely feasible in principle. There's a fallacy in Dave's analogy of the two houses that are priced identically. Dave’s analogy assumes that two houses listed at the same price occupy the same economic position, but that collapses once transaction costs and incentives are made explicit. An FSBO seller, lacking a commission burden, has greater flexibility to negotiate price while preserving net proceeds, so equal list prices do not imply equal bargaining positions. Worse, in an agent-listed sale the party doing much of the negotiating does not bear the full cost of concessions: a $10,000 price reduction costs the seller the full amount but costs the realtor only a small fraction in lost commission. As Freakonomics observes, this principal–agent asymmetry systematically favors faster sales over higher prices, so reduced time on market does not, by itself, translate into a better deal for the seller.

If this seller is such a good negotiator and can sell his house, start with your new employer and ask for all or a percentage of selling costs to be reimbursed. Also even if the caller ends up paying a commission, it's a cost of selling and most likely tax deductible when moving for a new job which factors into this seller's profit calculations.  This caller is not equipped to sell his own house and Ramsey should have suggested asking the employer to cover moving expenses before the seller makes a decision to sell FSBO or List in MLS.
